questions.stem: As the incoming nucleotide is added to the growing chain (right), a two-phosphate molecule is released. What does hydrolysis of this released molecule provide?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The template information for base selection

choices.label: B
choices.text: The hydrogen bonds holding the strands antiparallel

choices.label: C
choices.text: The signal that terminates strand synthesis

choices.label: D
choices.text: The thermodynamic driving force that makes chain elongation favorable

choices.label: E
choices.text: The methyl group added to the new base

explanation.summary: Nucleotides are added as triphosphates; cleavage and subsequent hydrolysis of the released pyrophosphate is highly exergonic and pulls the polymerization reaction forward.
explanation.whyCorrect: Nucleotides are added as triphosphates; cleavage and subsequent hydrolysis of the released pyrophosphate is highly exergonic and pulls the polymerization reaction forward.

questions.stem: The modified nucleotide joined by an unusual 5'-to-5' linkage at the start of the transcript serves what function for the mature mRNA?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It provides the anticodon that reads the first codon

choices.label: B
choices.text: It signals the site where the poly-A tail is added

choices.label: C
choices.text: It creates the branch point for lariat formation

choices.label: D
choices.text: It marks introns for removal by the spliceosome

choices.label: E
choices.text: It protects the mRNA from degradation and promotes ribosome binding for translation

explanation.summary: The 5' cap (7-methylguanosine linked 5'-to-5') stabilizes the transcript against exonucleases and is recognized by initiation factors to promote translation.
explanation.whyCorrect: The 5' cap (7-methylguanosine linked 5'-to-5') stabilizes the transcript against exonucleases and is recognized by initiation factors to promote translation.

questions.stem: The complex assembling on the primary transcript catalyzes which event, releasing the looped intermediate shown at the bottom?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Reverse transcription of RNA into DNA

choices.label: B
choices.text: Addition of the 5' cap to the transcript

choices.label: C
choices.text: Removal of the intron and joining of the flanking exons

choices.label: D
choices.text: Translation of the mRNA into protein

choices.label: E
choices.text: Attachment of the poly-A tail at the 3' end

explanation.summary: The spliceosome (built from snRNPs) cleaves at the 5' and 3' splice sites, excises the intron as a lariat, and ligates the two exons, an essential step in mRNA maturation.
explanation.whyCorrect: The spliceosome (built from snRNPs) cleaves at the 5' and 3' splice sites, excises the intron as a lariat, and ligates the two exons, an essential step in mRNA maturation.

questions.stem: The three different mRNAs and proteins produced from one hnRNA on the right illustrate a process that provides what benefit to the genome?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It lets a single gene generate multiple distinct protein products

choices.label: B
choices.text: It corrects mutations before translation occurs

choices.label: C
choices.text: It converts introns directly into functional proteins

choices.label: D
choices.text: It permanently deletes exons from the genomic DNA

choices.label: E
choices.text: It doubles the amount of DNA before cell division

explanation.summary: Alternative splicing joins different combinations of exons from the same primary transcript, so one gene can encode several protein isoforms, greatly expanding proteome diversity.
explanation.whyCorrect: Alternative splicing joins different combinations of exons from the same primary transcript, so one gene can encode several protein isoforms, greatly expanding proteome diversity.

questions.stem: This chart maps triplets to amino acids. What key property of this code is demonstrated by several different triplets specifying the same amino acid?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ambiguity of the code

choices.label: B
choices.text: Degeneracy (redundancy) of the genetic code

choices.label: C
choices.text: Overlapping reading of the code

choices.label: D
choices.text: Non-universality of the code

choices.label: E
choices.text: Proofreading by the ribosome

explanation.summary: Because most amino acids are encoded by more than one codon (e.g., six for leucine), the code is described as degenerate/redundant, which buffers many point mutations against changing the protein.
explanation.whyCorrect: Because most amino acids are encoded by more than one codon (e.g., six for leucine), the code is described as degenerate/redundant, which buffers many point mutations against changing the protein.

questions.stem: During elongation on this structure, through which of the three internal sites does a charged tRNA first enter before peptide bond formation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The peptidyl (P) site

choices.label: B
choices.text: The exit (E) site

choices.label: C
choices.text: The aminoacyl (A) site

choices.label: D
choices.text: The mRNA-binding site of the large subunit

choices.label: E
choices.text: The 60S catalytic center

explanation.summary: Incoming aminoacyl-tRNAs enter the A site; the growing peptide is held at the P site and transferred to the A-site amino acid, after which deacylated tRNA leaves through the E site.
explanation.whyCorrect: Incoming aminoacyl-tRNAs enter the A site; the growing peptide is held at the P site and transferred to the A-site amino acid, after which deacylated tRNA leaves through the E site.

questions.stem: The molecule diagrammed on the left (with an acceptor stem, D loop, T loop, and anticodon loop) is shown 'charged' on the right. What does the charged form on the right directly enable during protein synthesis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Addition of a 5' cap to the growing mRNA

choices.label: B
choices.text: Splicing of introns out of the primary transcript

choices.label: C
choices.text: Synthesis of the mRNA template from a DNA strand

choices.label: D
choices.text: Formation of the small and large ribosomal subunits

choices.label: E
choices.text: Delivery of a specific amino acid to the ribosome via anticodon-codon base pairing

explanation.summary: The cloverleaf molecule is a tRNA. When 'charged,' an amino acid is esterified to its 3' acceptor end; its anticodon then reads the complementary mRNA codon (with wobble at the third position), delivering the correct amino acid to the ribosomal A site during translation.
explanation.whyCorrect: The cloverleaf molecule is a tRNA. When 'charged,' an amino acid is esterified to its 3' acceptor end; its anticodon then reads the complementary mRNA codon (with wobble at the third position), delivering the correct amino acid to the ribosomal A site during translation.

questions.stem: The figure shows a ribosome whose emerging N-terminal sequence is bound by a cytosolic particle, docked to a receptor on the ER membrane, with the nascent chain threaded through a translocation channel and later cleaved. What is the functional purpose of the events shown?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Co-translational targeting and import of secretory/membrane proteins into the ER

choices.label: B
choices.text: Import of proteins into the mitochondrial matrix

choices.label: C
choices.text: Degradation of misfolded proteins by the proteasome

choices.label: D
choices.text: Nuclear export of mature mRNA transcripts

choices.label: E
choices.text: Retrograde transport of proteins from Golgi to ER

explanation.summary: The signal recognition particle (SRP) binds the hydrophobic signal peptide on the emerging chain, halts translation, and docks to the SRP receptor. Translation resumes through the translocon, threading the protein into the ER lumen; a signal peptidase then cleaves the signal sequence.
explanation.whyCorrect: The signal recognition particle (SRP) binds the hydrophobic signal peptide on the emerging chain, halts translation, and docks to the SRP receptor. Translation resumes through the translocon, threading the protein into the ER lumen; a signal peptidase then cleaves the signal sequence.

questions.stem: The structure shows a central carbon bonded to a hydrogen, a positively charged amino group, a negatively charged carboxyl group, and an R group. What class of biological molecule is this the general structure of?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A steroid

choices.label: B
choices.text: A monosaccharide

choices.label: C
choices.text: A nucleotide

choices.label: D
choices.text: An amino acid

choices.label: E
choices.text: A fatty acid

explanation.summary: The central (alpha) carbon bonded to an amino group, a carboxyl group, a hydrogen, and a variable R side chain is the defining structure of an amino acid. The R group distinguishes the 20 standard amino acids; the charged groups reflect the zwitterionic form at physiologic pH.
explanation.whyCorrect: The central (alpha) carbon bonded to an amino group, a carboxyl group, a hydrogen, and a variable R side chain is the defining structure of an amino acid. The R group distinguishes the 20 standard amino acids; the charged groups reflect the zwitterionic form at physiologic pH.

questions.stem: Two amino acids combine to form the product on the right, releasing the small molecule shown at the bottom. What type of reaction links the two residues, and what is the byproduct?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A condensation (dehydration) reaction that releases a molecule of water

choices.label: B
choices.text: A redox reaction that releases carbon dioxide

choices.label: C
choices.text: A phosphorylation reaction that releases ADP

choices.label: D
choices.text: A hydrolysis reaction that consumes a molecule of water

choices.label: E
choices.text: A decarboxylation reaction that releases ammonia

explanation.summary: The peptide bond forms by condensation: the carboxyl group of one amino acid joins the amino group of the next, releasing one water molecule (dehydration synthesis). The reverse, hydrolysis, breaks the bond by adding water.
explanation.whyCorrect: The peptide bond forms by condensation: the carboxyl group of one amino acid joins the amino group of the next, releasing one water molecule (dehydration synthesis). The reverse, hydrolysis, breaks the bond by adding water.

questions.stem: This molecule pairs a nonpolar hydrocarbon chain with a charged carboxyl head group. What key physical property does this dual character give the molecule?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It is completely nonpolar and inert

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is a strong reducing agent

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is amphipathic

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is fully water-soluble

choices.label: E
choices.text: It is optically inactive

explanation.summary: A fatty acid has a hydrophobic tail and a hydrophilic (charged) carboxylate head, making it amphipathic. This property lets fatty acids and their derivatives form micelles and membrane bilayers.
explanation.whyCorrect: A fatty acid has a hydrophobic tail and a hydrophilic (charged) carboxylate head, making it amphipathic. This property lets fatty acids and their derivatives form micelles and membrane bilayers.

questions.stem: The molecule on the left has a straight chain while the one on the right is bent. What structural feature accounts for the bend, and how does it affect membrane packing?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A trans double bond that tightens packing and raises melting point

choices.label: B
choices.text: A cis double bond causes a kink that loosens packing and increases fluidity

choices.label: C
choices.text: A branched methyl group added by chain elongation

choices.label: D
choices.text: A hydroxyl group that forms hydrogen bonds

choices.label: E
choices.text: An extra carboxyl group creating a second polar head

explanation.summary: The straight molecule is a saturated fatty acid; the bent one is unsaturated. A cis double bond introduces a rigid kink that prevents tight packing, lowering the melting point and increasing membrane fluidity.
explanation.whyCorrect: The straight molecule is a saturated fatty acid; the bent one is unsaturated. A cis double bond introduces a rigid kink that prevents tight packing, lowering the melting point and increasing membrane fluidity.

questions.stem: These three related molecules are the products of hepatic fatty acid overflow. Which of them is technically NOT a ketone despite being grouped with the others?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Acetone

choices.label: B
choices.text: All three are true ketones

choices.label: C
choices.text: None contains a carboxyl group

choices.label: D
choices.text: Beta-hydroxybutyrate

choices.label: E
choices.text: Acetoacetate

explanation.summary: The three ketone bodies are acetoacetate, acetone, and beta-hydroxybutyrate. Beta-hydroxybutyrate carries a hydroxyl (not a carbonyl/ketone) group, so it is chemically not a ketone, even though it is counted as a ketone body.
explanation.whyCorrect: The three ketone bodies are acetoacetate, acetone, and beta-hydroxybutyrate. Beta-hydroxybutyrate carries a hydroxyl (not a carbonyl/ketone) group, so it is chemically not a ketone, even though it is counted as a ketone body.

questions.stem: This Prussian-blue-stained marrow shows the arrowed cell with a ring of blue iron granules encircling the nucleus. What is this cell?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Basophilic stippled erythrocyte

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ringed sideroblast

choices.label: C
choices.text: Heinz body

choices.label: D
choices.text: Howell-Jolly body

choices.label: E
choices.text: Target cell

explanation.summary: A ringed sideroblast is an erythroid precursor with iron-laden mitochondria forming a perinuclear ring (Prussian blue positive), diagnostic of sideroblastic anemia.
explanation.whyCorrect: A ringed sideroblast is an erythroid precursor with iron-laden mitochondria forming a perinuclear ring (Prussian blue positive), diagnostic of sideroblastic anemia.

questions.stem: The two arms joined at the centromere on the right-hand chromosome are genetically identical copies. During the normal cell cycle they are generated by which event?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Transcription of chromosomal genes

choices.label: B
choices.text: Random assortment of homologs

choices.label: C
choices.text: DNA replication during S phase

choices.label: D
choices.text: Crossing over in prophase I

choices.label: E
choices.text: Nondisjunction in anaphase

explanation.summary: Sister chromatids are the two identical copies of a chromosome produced by DNA replication in S phase; they remain joined at the centromere until separated in anaphase.
explanation.whyCorrect: Sister chromatids are the two identical copies of a chromosome produced by DNA replication in S phase; they remain joined at the centromere until separated in anaphase.

questions.stem: The complete division cycle diagrammed here yields daughter cells with which chromosomal content relative to the original parent cell?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Two diploid cells genetically identical to the parent

choices.label: B
choices.text: Two triploid cells

choices.label: C
choices.text: Two haploid cells identical to the parent

choices.label: D
choices.text: Four haploid, genetically unique cells

choices.label: E
choices.text: Four diploid cells with recombination

explanation.summary: Mitosis produces two daughter cells that are diploid (2n) and genetically identical to the parent, in contrast to meiosis which yields four genetically unique haploid cells.
explanation.whyCorrect: Mitosis produces two daughter cells that are diploid (2n) and genetically identical to the parent, in contrast to meiosis which yields four genetically unique haploid cells.

questions.stem: The two-stage division sequence shown, which includes pairing of homologous chromosomes and crossing over, ultimately produces which final products?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Two diploid identical cells

choices.label: B
choices.text: Four genetically unique haploid gametes

choices.label: C
choices.text: One diploid and one haploid cell

choices.label: D
choices.text: Four diploid unique cells

choices.label: E
choices.text: Two haploid identical cells

explanation.summary: Meiosis I separates homologous chromosomes (reductional) and meiosis II separates sister chromatids, yielding four haploid cells that are genetically unique due to crossing over and independent assortment.
explanation.whyCorrect: Meiosis I separates homologous chromosomes (reductional) and meiosis II separates sister chromatids, yielding four haploid cells that are genetically unique due to crossing over and independent assortment.
